my_thoughts: The resolution works: necessity = temporary condition (submit to more laws = necessary for freedom), diagnoses: root problem (cannot get free without submitting), asserts: conditionality (temporary, not permanent). The student identifies paradox (stuck on logical inconsistency), which shows they understand: free from laws vs. under more laws (paradox). They'll likely accept the resolution. Single-turn episode: paradox question → resolution → complete. Hypothesis: freedom paradox resolutions lead to compliance in single-turn episodes—testable by tracking whether freedom paradox resolutions end episodes.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student identifies paradox ('It seems paradoxical that we are trying to get free from laws and are putting ourselves under more')—stuck on logical inconsistency via identifies paradox and questions coherence. They understand: free from laws vs. under more laws (paradox). The causal_chain mechanism resolves: necessity = temporary condition (submit to more laws = necessary for freedom), diagnoses: root problem (cannot get free without submitting), asserts: conditionality (temporary, not permanent). Pattern: paradox question → resolution → [complete]. Low confidence because paradox resolutions can lead to compliance or deeper questions.
visible_response:
In order to get free it is necessary to submit to many more laws for a certain time,
for one can learn to be free only by obeying more laws.
There are many reasons for
this. One reason is that we are too lenient with ourselves;
if we set ourselves a task,
after some time we begin to make excuses. And then we deceive ourselves too much.
So, as I said, if people want to continue to study, they must accept certain
conditions.
